news_summary: Collaborative global observations reveal detailed insights into the 2024 total solar eclipse, while the Webb telescope and recent research shed new light on Uranus’ atmosphere and the dynamic history of Saturn’s rings.
news_summary_fr: Des observations collaboratives mondiales révèlent des informations détaillées sur l'éclipse solaire totale de 2024, tandis que le télescope Webb et des recherches récentes apportent un nouvel éclairage sur l'atmosphère d'Uranus et l'histoire dynamique des anneaux de Saturne.
news_summary_es: Las observaciones colaborativas a nivel mundial revelan información detallada sobre el eclipse solar total de 2024, mientras que el telescopio Webb y las investigaciones recientes arrojan nueva luz sobre la atmósfera de Urano y la dinámica historia de los anillos de Saturno.

articles.title: New Volunteer Data from 143 Observatories Unveils the 2024 Total Solar Eclipse
articles.summary: On April 8, 2024, volunteers participating in NASA’s Eclipse Megamovie citizen science project all around the United States hurried to photograph the solar eclipse with the latest, greatest equipment, capturing groundbreaking images of the Sun’s corona.
articles.content:
On April 8, 2024, volunteers participating in NASA’s Eclipse Megamovie citizen science project all around the United States hurried to photograph the solar eclipse with the latest, greatest equipment, capturing groundbreaking images of the Sun’s corona.

Now, the Eclipse Megamovie team has released the remarkable new dataset that resulted from this effort — the first-ever, white-light eclipse dataset with calibration frames, spanning more than a cumulative hour and a half of observations of the solar corona. This data, which includes 52,469 total photographs uploaded by project volunteers, is now live: https://eclipsemegamovie.org/database. The data include contributions from 143 unique, mobile, volunteer-led “observatories” – people with cameras charged with taking precise images of the eclipse, taking extra steps to allow the painstaking calibration required to reveal how the corona evolves from one person's view to the next. Researchers around the world can now use these observations to identify solar jets leaving the Sun's surface and study how solar plumes grow and develop. The public can also peruse and download all of this data, which is highly accessible and searchable by observatory name and location.

"Thank you for all you do and have done for us,” said Eclipse Megamovie volunteer Jessi McKenna. “Everyone in the group has been amazingly supportive of each other. And those who are running things are always so obviously appreciative of everyone who has contributed to the project."

The files include data at three different levels of processing, from raw (level 1) data to calibrated (level 3) data, in a format called FITS, or Flexible Image Transport System. It is the standard astronomical data format used by NASA and the International Astronomical Union. Of the 143 unique observatories involved, 28 observatories had clear skies, sufficient calibration frames, and enough unique exposure times to create calibrated level 3 images.

The Eclipse Megamovie team at Sonoma State University and the University of California, Berkeley and collaborators at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center began working together long before the eclipse to construct this database, together with EdEon STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, & Mathematics) Learning programmer Troy Wilson. But crucially, Eclipse Megamovie 2024 was made possible because of hundreds of volunteers who journeyed into the path of the April 8, 2024 total solar eclipse with their cameras, patience, and curiosity.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant citizen science project involving hundreds of volunteers capturing and compiling a large, calibrated dataset of the 2024 total solar eclipse. This dataset enables researchers worldwide to study solar phenomena in unprecedented detail, representing a meaningful scientific advancement with broad public engagement and educational impact.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers create detailed photo database of 2024 solar eclipse
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On April 8, 2024, a total solar eclipse crossed the United States. During this event, the Moon completely covered the Sun for a short time, revealing the Sun’s outer atmosphere called the corona.

**What happened** – Hundreds of volunteers across the country took part in NASA’s Eclipse Megamovie project. They used cameras to take tens of thousands of pictures of the eclipse. In total, 143 groups of volunteers, called observatories, collected and shared 52,469 photos. These images were carefully processed to create a detailed, calibrated dataset showing how the Sun’s corona changes during the eclipse.

**Impact** – This is the first time such a large and well-calibrated set of white-light eclipse photos has been made available. Scientists can use this data to study features like solar jets and plumes that come from the Sun’s surface. It helps researchers learn more about how the Sun’s atmosphere behaves. The public can also explore and download the photos, making this a unique mix of science and citizen involvement.

**What's next step** – Researchers worldwide will analyze the database to better understand solar activity. The project also sets a new standard for combining volunteer efforts with scientific research, encouraging more citizen science projects in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A large team of volunteers created the most detailed photo collection of the 2024 solar eclipse, helping scientists study the Sun’s corona like never before.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles créent une base de données photo détaillée de l’éclipse solaire 2024
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 8 avril 2024, une éclipse solaire totale a traversé les États-Unis. Pendant cet événement, la Lune a complètement caché le Soleil pendant un court moment, révélant la couronne solaire, l’atmosphère extérieure du Soleil.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des centaines de bénévoles à travers le pays ont participé au projet Eclipse Megamovie de la NASA. Ils ont utilisé des appareils photo pour prendre des dizaines de milliers de photos de l’éclipse. Au total, 143 groupes de bénévoles, appelés observatoires, ont collecté et partagé 52 469 photos. Ces images ont été soigneusement traitées pour créer une base de données détaillée et calibrée montrant comment la couronne solaire change pendant l’éclipse.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un ensemble aussi large et bien calibré de photos en lumière blanche d’une éclipse est rendu disponible. Les scientifiques peuvent utiliser ces données pour étudier des phénomènes comme les jets solaires et les panaches qui viennent de la surface du Soleil. Cela aide à mieux comprendre le comportement de l’atmosphère solaire. Le public peut aussi explorer et télécharger les photos, ce qui fait de ce projet un mélange unique de science et de participation citoyenne.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs du monde entier analyseront cette base de données pour mieux comprendre l’activité solaire. Le projet établit aussi une nouvelle norme pour combiner les efforts des bénévoles avec la recherche scientifique, encourageant ainsi plus de projets de science citoyenne.

**Phrase clé** – Une grande équipe de bénévoles a créé la collection photo la plus détaillée de l’éclipse solaire 2024, aidant les scientifiques à étudier la couronne du Soleil comme jamais auparavant.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios crean una base de datos fotográfica detallada del eclipse solar 2024
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 8 de abril de 2024, un eclipse solar total cruzó Estados Unidos. Durante este evento, la Luna cubrió completamente el Sol por un corto tiempo, mostrando la atmósfera exterior del Sol llamada corona.

**Qué pasó** – Cientos de voluntarios en todo el país participaron en el proyecto Eclipse Megamovie de la NASA. Usaron cámaras para tomar decenas de miles de fotos del eclipse. En total, 143 grupos de voluntarios, llamados observatorios, recolectaron y compartieron 52,469 fotos. Estas imágenes fueron procesadas cuidadosamente para crear un conjunto de datos detallado y calibrado que muestra cómo cambia la corona solar durante el eclipse.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que se ofrece un conjunto tan grande y bien calibrado de fotos en luz blanca de un eclipse. Los científicos pueden usar estos datos para estudiar características como chorros solares y plumas que salen de la superficie del Sol. Esto ayuda a entender mejor cómo se comporta la atmósfera solar. El público también puede explorar y descargar las fotos, haciendo de este un proyecto único que combina la ciencia con la participación ciudadana.

**Próximo paso** – Investigadores de todo el mundo analizarán la base de datos para comprender mejor la actividad solar. El proyecto también establece un nuevo estándar para combinar el esfuerzo de voluntarios con la investigación científica, alentando más proyectos de ciencia ciudadana en el futuro.

**Frase clave** – Un gran equipo de voluntarios creó la colección fotográfica más detallada del eclipse solar 2024, ayudando a los científicos a estudiar la corona del Sol como nunca antes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a network of glowing telescopes and cameras arranged like a constellation across a softly lit map of the United States, each device gently capturing a radiant, stylized solar eclipse with a luminous corona, symbolizing volunteer collaboration and the collective unveiling of the Sun’s hidden beauty.

articles.title: Webb Maps Uranus’ Upper Atmosphere
articles.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope provided the first vertical view of Uranus’s ionosphere in this image released on Feb. 19, 2026, revealing auroras shaped by its tilted magnetic field. Getting a look at the structure of the region where the atmosphere interacts strongly with the planet’s magnetic field is giving us the most detailed portrait […]
articles.content:
ESA/Webb, NASA, CSA, STScI, P. Tiranti, H. Melin, M. Zamani (ESA/Webb)

NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope provided the first vertical view of Uranus’s ionosphere in this image released on Feb. 19, 2026, revealing auroras shaped by its tilted magnetic field.

Getting a look at the structure of the region where the atmosphere interacts strongly with the planet’s magnetic field is giving us the most detailed portrait yet of where its auroras form, how the magnetic field influences them, and also data on how Uranus’s atmosphere has continued to cool since the 1990s.

Uranus has the strangest magnetosphere in the Solar System. It is tilted and offset from the planet’s rotation axis (and this planet already rolls around the Sun nearly on its side), which means auroras move across the surface in complex ways. Better understanding Uranus will give us insight into ice-giant planets and help us better characterize giant planets outside our Solar System.

Read more about this image.

Image credit: ESA/Webb, NASA, CSA, STScI, P. Tiranti, H. Melin, M. Zamani (ESA/Webb)
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement by NASA's James Webb Space Telescope providing the first vertical view of Uranus's ionosphere, revealing new insights into its auroras and magnetic field. This breakthrough enhances our understanding of ice-giant planets and has broad implications for planetary science and exoplanet characterization, representing a meaningful advancement in space exploration.
articles.personality_title: James Webb Telescope captures first vertical view of Uranus’ auroras
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Uranus is one of the ice-giant planets in our Solar System. It has a strange magnetic field that is tilted and does not line up with how the planet spins. This creates unusual auroras, which are bright lights in the sky caused by the planet’s magnetic field interacting with particles.

**What happened** – On February 19, 2026, NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope took the first vertical image of Uranus’s ionosphere. The ionosphere is the upper part of the atmosphere where charged particles interact with the magnetic field. This new image shows how the auroras form and move because of Uranus’s tilted magnetic field.

**Impact** – This is the most detailed look we have ever had at the area where Uranus’s atmosphere and magnetic field meet. Scientists can now better understand why Uranus’s auroras behave differently from those on other planets. The data also shows that Uranus’s atmosphere has been cooling since the 1990s. Learning about Uranus helps us understand other similar planets, including ones around other stars.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this new information to study more about ice-giant planets and their magnetic fields. It will also help improve how we study giant planets outside our Solar System, called exoplanets, by comparing their atmospheres and magnetic behavior.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The James Webb Space Telescope has given us the first clear vertical view of Uranus’s auroras, revealing how its unique magnetic field shapes them and improving our knowledge of ice-giant planets.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb capture la première vue verticale des aurores d’Uranus
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Uranus est une des planètes géantes de glace du Système solaire. Son champ magnétique est étrange car il est incliné et ne correspond pas à l’axe de rotation de la planète. Cela crée des aurores inhabituelles, des lumières brillantes dans le ciel causées par l’interaction entre le champ magnétique et les particules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 19 février 2026, le télescope spatial James Webb de la NASA a pris la première image verticale de l’ionosphère d’Uranus. L’ionosphère est la partie supérieure de l’atmosphère où les particules chargées interagissent avec le champ magnétique. Cette nouvelle image montre comment les aurores se forment et se déplacent à cause du champ magnétique incliné d’Uranus.

**Impact** – C’est le regard le plus détaillé que nous ayons jamais eu sur la zone où l’atmosphère d’Uranus et son champ magnétique se rencontrent. Les scientifiques peuvent mieux comprendre pourquoi les aurores d’Uranus sont différentes de celles des autres planètes. Les données montrent aussi que l’atmosphère d’Uranus se refroidit depuis les années 1990. Étudier Uranus aide à mieux comprendre d’autres planètes similaires, y compris celles autour d’autres étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront ces nouvelles informations pour étudier davantage les planètes géantes de glace et leurs champs magnétiques. Cela aidera aussi à améliorer l’étude des planètes géantes en dehors de notre Système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, en comparant leurs atmosphères et leurs comportements magnétiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le télescope James Webb nous offre la première vue verticale claire des aurores d’Uranus, révélant comment son champ magnétique unique les façonne et améliorant notre connaissance des planètes géantes de glace.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio James Webb captura la primera vista vertical de las auroras de Urano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Urano es uno de los planetas gigantes de hielo en nuestro Sistema Solar. Tiene un campo magnético extraño que está inclinado y no coincide con el eje de rotación del planeta. Esto crea auroras inusuales, luces brillantes en el cielo causadas por la interacción del campo magnético con partículas.

**Qué pasó** – El 19 de febrero de 2026, el telescopio espacial James Webb de la NASA tomó la primera imagen vertical de la ionosfera de Urano. La ionosfera es la parte superior de la atmósfera donde las partículas cargadas interactúan con el campo magnético. Esta nueva imagen muestra cómo se forman y mueven las auroras debido al campo magnético inclinado de Urano.

**Impacto** – Esta es la vista más detallada que hemos tenido hasta ahora de la zona donde la atmósfera de Urano y su campo magnético se encuentran. Los científicos ahora pueden entender mejor por qué las auroras de Urano se comportan diferente a las de otros planetas. Los datos también muestran que la atmósfera de Urano se ha estado enfriando desde los años 90. Aprender sobre Urano nos ayuda a entender otros planetas similares, incluyendo los que están fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán esta nueva información para estudiar más sobre los planetas gigantes de hielo y sus campos magnéticos. También ayudará a mejorar cómo estudiamos los planetas gigantes fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar, llamados exoplanetas, comparando sus atmósferas y comportamientos magnéticos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El telescopio James Webb nos ha dado la primera vista vertical clara de las auroras de Urano, revelando cómo su campo magnético único las forma y mejorando nuestro conocimiento de los planetas gigantes de hielo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of Uranus viewed from the side, showing its tilted, rolling axis and a swirling, offset magnetic field illustrated as glowing, flowing lines shaping vibrant auroras that ripple across the planet’s upper atmosphere, with soft blues and greens blending naturally to evoke the cooling atmosphere and the dynamic interaction between magnetic forces and atmospheric layers.

articles.title: Saturn’s rings may have formed after a huge collision with Titan
articles.summary: Titan, the largest moon of Saturn, may have been even more instrumental to the system’s evolution than we thought, forming its rings, shaping its moons and even affecting the planet itself
articles.content:
Titan, the largest moon of Saturn, may have been even more instrumental to the system’s evolution than we thought, forming its rings, shaping its moons and even affecting the planet itself

Titan, Saturn’s largest moon, with the giant planet behind it in a view from the Cassini-Huygens spacecraft ZUMA Press, Inc./Alamy

The story of Saturn, its rings and moons, may have started with its largest moon, Titan. A collision between an early proto-Titan and a smaller object about 400 million years ago could have set into motion the series of events that formed Saturn’s iconic rings and altered both the planet’s wobble and the orbits of its moons.

The Saturn system is awash in mysteries. Its rings seem to be younger than expected, the planet’s wobble isn’t tied to the motion of Neptune as simulations have suggested it ought to be, and its small moon Iapetus has a strangely tilted orbit. Titan itself has strangely few craters and an oval, or eccentric, orbit.

A huge collision that created the Titan we see today could explain all of these elements. “This is sort of a grand unified theory that covers all of the major problems,” says Matija Ćuk at the SETI Institute in California, who led the research team behind this work. “We had some idea about each of them, but this might be how they relate in one story that can be tested.”

It starts with a hypothesised extra moon called Chrysalis in the outer reaches of the system, which was proposed in 2022 to explain how Saturn’s wobble got decoupled from Neptune. The idea was that Chrysalis got tossed towards Saturn and broke up to form the rings, destabilising Saturn’s wobble and Iapetus’s orbit in the process. However, Ćuk and his colleagues noticed that in simulations, the most likely outcome was that Chrysalis would collide with Titan.

That’s a problem, says Ćuk. “If there was a collision with Titan, it could not have become the rings.” So he and his team went about calculating what would happen if Chrysalis did smash into Titan. They found that such a collision about 400 million years ago would erase Titan’s craters and push its then-circular orbit to become elliptical, as well as creating a shower of debris. The smaller moon Hyperion could be a piece of that debris, which would explain why it is so much younger than Saturn’s other moons.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Then, over time, Titan’s changing orbit would have destabilised the small inner moons and sent them careening into one another, grinding each other down into the tiny particles that now make up Saturn’s rings. “It all starts from Titan and then trickles down to a second catastrophe in the inner system,” says Ćuk.

“If a collision with Titan 1.0 can explain many other things about the Saturn system, then I think that would really centre Titan as being pivotal to how we see the system today,” says Sarah Hörst at Johns Hopkins University in Maryland. “I appreciate the elegance of how many Saturn system problems it would solve at once.”

Evidence that could prove or rule out this scenario isn’t too far off. NASA’s Dragonfly mission, which is slated to launch in 2028 and arrive at Titan in 2034, will get a close look at Titan’s surface, which should help determine whether it did merge with Chrysalis. If so, we may finally understand some of the many oddities of Saturn.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific hypothesis that could unify and explain multiple mysteries about Saturn's system, including the formation of its rings and the evolution of its moons. This breakthrough enhances our understanding of planetary science and will be tested by an upcoming NASA mission, offering tangible progress in space exploration knowledge with broad scientific impact.
articles.personality_title: Titan's ancient collision may explain Saturn’s rings and moons
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Saturn is famous for its beautiful rings and many moons, but scientists have long wondered how these rings formed and why some moons behave strangely. Titan, the largest moon of Saturn, also has unusual features like few craters and an oval orbit.

**What happened** – Researchers suggest that about 400 million years ago, Titan collided with another moon called Chrysalis. This big crash could have wiped away Titan’s craters, changed its orbit, and created debris. Over time, this debris and other small moons may have broken down into the rings we see today. The collision might also explain why Saturn’s wobble and some moons’ orbits look unusual.

**Impact** – This idea links many puzzling facts about Saturn’s system into one story. Instead of separate explanations, the collision shows how Titan played a key role in shaping the planet’s rings and moons. It also helps explain why some moons are younger or have strange orbits, making Titan central to understanding Saturn.

**What's next step** – NASA’s Dragonfly mission, launching in 2028 and arriving at Titan in 2034, will study Titan’s surface closely. The data it collects can confirm if this collision really happened, helping scientists test this new explanation and learn more about Saturn’s history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A giant ancient crash involving Titan may have formed Saturn’s rings and shaped its moons, offering a new way to understand the planet’s unique system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une ancienne collision de Titan pourrait expliquer les anneaux et lunes de Saturne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Saturne est célèbre pour ses magnifiques anneaux et ses nombreuses lunes, mais les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps comment ces anneaux se sont formés et pourquoi certaines lunes ont des comportements étranges. Titan, la plus grande lune de Saturne, a aussi des caractéristiques inhabituelles, comme peu de cratères et une orbite ovale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs suggèrent qu’il y a environ 400 millions d’années, Titan a percuté une autre lune appelée Chrysalis. Ce grand impact aurait pu effacer les cratères de Titan, changer son orbite et créer des débris. Avec le temps, ces débris et d’autres petites lunes auraient pu se briser pour former les anneaux que nous voyons aujourd’hui. La collision pourrait aussi expliquer pourquoi le mouvement de Saturne et certaines orbites de lunes sont étranges.

**Impact** – Cette idée relie plusieurs faits mystérieux sur le système de Saturne en une seule histoire. Au lieu d’explications séparées, la collision montre que Titan a joué un rôle clé dans la formation des anneaux et des lunes. Cela aide aussi à comprendre pourquoi certaines lunes sont plus jeunes ou ont des orbites particulières, faisant de Titan un élément central pour comprendre Saturne.

**Prochaine étape** – La mission Dragonfly de la NASA, qui sera lancée en 2028 et arrivera sur Titan en 2034, étudiera de près la surface de Titan. Les données recueillies permettront de confirmer si cette collision a vraiment eu lieu, aidant les scientifiques à tester cette nouvelle explication et à mieux connaître l’histoire de Saturne.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une ancienne collision géante impliquant Titan pourrait avoir formé les anneaux de Saturne et façonné ses lunes, offrant une nouvelle clé pour comprendre ce système unique.
articles.personality_title_es: Una antigua colisión de Titán podría explicar los anillos y lunas de Saturno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Saturno es famoso por sus hermosos anillos y muchas lunas, pero los científicos han tenido dudas sobre cómo se formaron los anillos y por qué algunas lunas actúan de manera extraña. Titán, la luna más grande de Saturno, también tiene características inusuales, como pocos cráteres y una órbita ovalada.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores sugieren que hace unos 400 millones de años, Titán chocó con otra luna llamada Crisalis. Este gran choque pudo borrar los cráteres de Titán, cambiar su órbita y crear escombros. Con el tiempo, estos escombros y otras lunas pequeñas podrían haberse desintegrado para formar los anillos que vemos hoy. La colisión también podría explicar por qué el movimiento de Saturno y algunas órbitas de lunas son extrañas.

**Impacto** – Esta idea une muchos datos misteriosos sobre el sistema de Saturno en una sola historia. En lugar de explicaciones separadas, la colisión muestra que Titán jugó un papel clave en la formación de los anillos y las lunas. También ayuda a entender por qué algunas lunas son más jóvenes o tienen órbitas particulares, haciendo de Titán un elemento central para comprender Saturno.

**Próximo paso** – La misión Dragonfly de la NASA, que se lanzará en 2028 y llegará a Titán en 2034, estudiará de cerca la superficie de Titán. Los datos que recoja podrán confirmar si esta colisión realmente ocurrió, ayudando a los científicos a probar esta nueva explicación y aprender más sobre la historia de Saturno.

**Frase clave** – Una antigua colisión gigante que involucró a Titán podría haber formado los anillos de Saturno y moldeado sus lunas, ofreciendo una nueva forma de entender este sistema único.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of Saturn with its large, glowing moon Titan in the foreground, depicted as a smooth, craterless orb gently colliding with a smaller, fragmented moon, while delicate, shimmering rings form gradually around Saturn from swirling debris, all rendered in soft natural hues of gold, amber, and dusty blues.
